questions.text: I find that people sometimes describe social situations to me as if explaining a movie I watched with the sound off.
I find that my best days are the ones where I spent most of my time helping others.
I believe that patience and collective input lead to wiser choices.
I feel that when I take care of my own needs, I am better equipped for everything else.
I tend to let the group define its own boundaries collectively.
I believe that recognizing my own worth and acting accordingly is a sign of health.
I often intuit the backstory between people I have just met based on how they interact.
I shop and pack based on what occurs to me in the moment rather than from a list.
I find that I can engage fully with someone's difficult situation without it altering my emotional baseline.
I feel that I have a long fuse — it takes a lot to provoke a strong emotional response in me.
I feel responsible for creating the conditions that will lead to my desired future.
I often set the vision and expect others to execute it.
I think that evaluating distressing situations through a detached, systematic lens is the most responsible way to determine the right course of action.
My baseline emotional state is one of calm readiness.
I tend to be the first person in a room to react emotionally to what's happening.
I feel that the obligation to take others' suffering seriously is one of the few moral commitments I would maintain under any circumstances.
I find that I am usually focused on the content of a conversation rather than its politics.
I hold the view that finding shortcuts and faster paths is a form of intelligence.
